%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.8-2 Complex Number Identities — Compact Reference

> **Section Objective:** Master the four most testable complex-number skills: read Euler form, convert \(a + jb\) into \(r\) and \(\theta\), apply power rules correctly, and multiply complex numbers in exponential form.

This subsection is a **compact reference sheet**, not a full derivation lesson. The formulas here appear constantly inside larger signal-processing, phasor, and differential-equation problems — and students lose points not because they forget the big idea, but because they misread a sign or pick the wrong quadrant for \(\theta\).

This lesson will translate each formula into its exact exam meaning. Work through it efficiently: every block targets a specific mistake that costs marks.


## 1. Euler Form and Rectangular Form Mean the Same Complex Number

Euler's formula is the **bridge** between angle language and coordinate language. The expression \(e^{\pm j\theta}\) is not abstract — it describes a point on a unit circle, and its real and imaginary parts are simply \(\cos\theta\) and \(\pm\sin\theta\).

When you write \(a + jb = re^{j\theta}\), you are saying the same point can be described two ways: by its horizontal component \(a\) and vertical component \(b\), or by its distance from the origin \(r\) and its direction \(\theta\). The symbol \(j\) marks the imaginary-axis direction — it tells you which component is vertical.

### KEY INSIGHT

\(r\) is the straight-line distance from the origin to the point. \(\theta\) is the angle measured counterclockwise from the positive real axis.

### EXAM TRAP

\(\theta = \tan^{-1}(b/a)\) gives only a **reference angle**. If \(a < 0\) or \(b < 0\), the point is not in the first quadrant, and you must correct \(\theta\) using the signs of \(a\) and \(b\) to identify the true quadrant. Skipping this step is one of the most common sources of lost marks.

$$e^{\pm j\theta} = \cos\theta \pm j\sin\theta$$
*This formula converts angle-based exponential notation into real-axis and imaginary-axis components, with the sign on the \(\sin\theta\) term matching exactly the sign in the exponent.*

$$a + jb = r e^{j\theta}, \qquad r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}, \qquad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$
*Here \(r\) is the magnitude (distance from the origin) and \(\theta\) is the angle (direction from the positive real axis) of the complex number. **Trap:** \(\tan^{-1}(b/a)\) yields only a reference angle — always check the signs of \(a\) and \(b\) to confirm the correct quadrant before finalizing \(\theta\).*

## 2. Powers, Products, and Special-Angle Shortcuts

Three rules belong together because they are all about **what happens to \(r\) and \(\theta\) under operations**.

**Powers:** \((re^{j\theta})^k\) raises the magnitude to the \(k\)th power and multiplies the angle by \(k\). Both change.

**Products:** \((r_1 e^{j\theta_1})(r_2 e^{j\theta_2})\) multiplies the magnitudes and adds the angles. The magnitudes never add.

**Special angles:** \(e^{\pm j\pi/2} = \pm j\) and \(e^{\pm jn\pi}\) equals \(1\) when \(n\) is even and \(-1\) when \(n\) is odd. These are speed shortcuts — memorize them.

### EXAM TRAP

Two easy mistakes: (1) **adding magnitudes** instead of multiplying them in a product, and (2) applying the parity rule by looking at the \(\pm\) sign instead of the value of \(n\). The parity rule depends entirely on whether \(n\) is even or odd — the sign in the exponent does not change the result because \(\sin(n\pi) = 0\) for all integers \(n\).

---

$$\left(r e^{j\theta}\right)^k = r^k e^{jk\theta}, \qquad \left(r_1 e^{j\theta_1}\right)\left(r_2 e^{j\theta_2}\right)=r_1 r_2 e^{j(\theta_1+\theta_2)}$$
*For a power, one angle is scaled by \(k\) and one magnitude is raised to \(k\); for a product of two complex numbers, the two angles add and the two magnitudes multiply — never add.*

$$e^{\pm j\pi/2}=\pm j, \qquad e^{\pm j n\pi}=\begin{cases}1, & n\text{ even}\\-1, & n\text{ odd}\end{cases}$$
*These are speed shortcuts for the most common angles in exam problems; note that both \(e^{+jn\pi}\) and \(e^{-jn\pi}\) give the same real result because \(\sin(n\pi) = 0\) for every integer \(n\), so the sign in the exponent is irrelevant here.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Euler form \(e^{\pm j\theta} = \cos\theta \pm j\sin\theta\) bridges angle notation and rectangular components; sign in exponent matches sign on \(j\sin\theta\).
- Convert \(a + jb\) to \(re^{j\theta}\) using \(r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\); always verify the quadrant from the signs of \(a\) and \(b\) before finalizing \(\theta\).
- Powers raise \(r\) to \(k\) and multiply \(\theta\) by \(k\); products multiply magnitudes and add angles — never add magnitudes.

*In the next section we will use these formulas as tools inside larger problems.*
